Examples found during review include FEAT-006 Agent Service overview/responsibilities, FEAT-010 Layer 1 wording, and cli/internal/skills/ddx/reference/agents.md persona/quorum/help examples. Parallel copied skill docs under .agents/skills/ddx and .claude/skills/ddx contain the same stale vocabulary.\\n\\nWhy this matters:\\nThe docs are executable guidance for agents. Leaving legacy command names and flags in skills will keep agents routing work through the old surfaces even if the implementation adds top-level run/try/work.\\n\\nRequired fix:\\nMake the public command taxonomy bright-line and uniform across feature docs, skill docs, and CLI help: ddx run is the single agent execution primitive, ddx try layers retry/review policy over run, and ddx work layers bead orchestration over try. Agent-internal service commands may exist only as hidden/internal plumbing and must not be documented as workflow commands.\\n\\nAcceptance:\\n- FEAT-006 and FEAT-010 no longer describe ddx agent run/execute-bead/execute-loop as public orchestration commands.\\n- DDx skill references in cli/internal/skills/ddx, .agents/skills/ddx, and .claude/skills/ddx use ddx run/try/work consistently.\\n- Quorum/compare/persona examples are either migrated to the simplified surface or removed if they imply DDx harness routing.\\n- A docs/skill grep test or validation step fails on stale public references to ddx agent run, execute-bead, execute-loop, --harnesses, or provider-routing examples outside explicit migration notes.\",\"id\":\"ddx-52ce6249\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"helix\",\"phase:build\",\"review-finding\",\"area:docs\",\"area:cli\"],\"parent\":\"ddx-deb9be7e\",\"priority\":1,\"spec_id\":\"review:power-passthrough-boundary-stale-commands\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"review finding: remove stale ddx agent run and execute-bead command guidance\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-30T03:50:03.186901956Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:282:{\"acceptance\":\"The DDx docs define `ddx agent execute-bead` as a clean-worktree workspace transform from `W` to `W'`; the bead is specified as the executable transform contract rather than a vague tracker ticket; DDx docs no longer assign governing-artifact resolution or prompt construction to DDx at runtime; a generic bead execution-readiness validation surface is specified with clear DDx-owned vs HELIX-owned checks; the docs define the success/failure state machine including structural validation, required post-run checks, preserve-on-failure evidence, and rebase+fast-forward landing on success; and the contract explicitly remains valid for future multi-project `ddx server` worker pools rather than assuming a single served repo\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-09T22:00:19.106711764Z\",\"description\":\"\\u003ccontext-digest\\u003e\\nReview area: execute-bead semantics, executable bead contracts, readiness validation, and DDx/HELIX boundary ownership. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, FEAT-013, SD-012, server-driven execution discussions, and cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go.\\n\\u003c/context-digest\\u003e\\nDefine DDx bead execution around the functional model `W' = execute(bead, W)`, where `W` is a clean git-backed workspace input state and `ddx agent execute-bead` materializes that state in an isolated worktree before mutation.\\n\\n## Required design outcomes\\n1. Clarify that `ddx agent execute-bead` starts from a resolved base revision, treats the caller's dirty worktree as outside the transform, checkpoints it separately, and executes only against the isolated worktree state.\\n2. Define the bead as the executable transform contract: the bead must carry the instruction/prompt, acceptance, declared context inputs, execution policy, landing/preservation policy, and post-run validation policy needed for replayable execution.\\n3. Unwind DDx designs that currently imply DDx derives governing artifacts, prompt construction, or workflow policy from the live document graph at execution time. DDx should provide composable graph/exec tools, not hidden workflow orchestration.\\n4. Define a generic DDx execution-readiness validation surface for beads, such as `ddx bead validate --execution` or equivalent inspection semantics, that checks structural executability without embedding HELIX-specific policy.\\n5. Specify the success/failure state machine around execution: structural validation, agent execution, post-run validity checks / required executions, preserve-on-failure evidence semantics, and land-on-success semantics.\\n6. Make the automation boundary explicit: future humans, HELIX flows, or `ddx server` worker pools must consume the explicit bead contract and validator output rather than relying on hidden prompt assembly or runtime artifact inference.\\n7. Preserve room for multi-project server execution: the contract must not assume a single served repo or a single server process, and must remain valid when project-scoped worktrees are managed by a multi-project `ddx server`.\\n8. Make HELIX's responsibility explicit: HELIX should author the executable bead contract and create a new contract document in `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` that defines the DDx/HELIX boundary for executable bead execution. The contracts directory does not currently exist in this repo, so this work must establish it.\\n\\n## Spec and design scope\\n- Reconcile FEAT-006 agent-service workflow with the workspace-transform model\\n- Reconcile FEAT-010 execution-definition language so execution selection and required checks are explicit input or separately composed query output, not hidden bead-context derivation\\n- Preserve FEAT-013's substrate-not-orchestration boundary while still supporting async server-managed worker pools\\n- Align SD-012 git-awareness wording with the actual clean-input/worktree model, checkpoint semantics, and preserve-vs-land outcomes\\n- Establish how the generic execution-readiness surface is consumed by future automation without embedding HELIX policy in DDx\\n\\n## Expected artifact work\\n- New contract under `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` for the executable bead boundary\\n- Updates to the affected feature / solution design docs so they all describe the same execution contract\\n- Follow-on design beads for server-managed supervision and multi-project server topology\\n- Follow-on implementation beads only after the contract is settled\",\"execution-eligible\":true,\"id\":\"ddx-5448b39c\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"helix\",\"phase:planning\",\"kind:architecture\",\"area:agent\",\"area:bead\",\"area:docs\"],\"priority\":0,\"spec-id\":\"FEAT-006\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"Define execute-bead workspace-transform contract and readiness validation\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-10T16:14:55.073044325Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:290:{\"acceptance\":\"FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-10T22:14:49.010697815Z\",\"description\":\"\\u003ccontext-digest\\u003e\\nReview area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\\n\\u003c/context-digest\\u003e\\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\\n\\n## Goals\\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\\n\\n## Required spec work\\n- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed\\n- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces\\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\\n\\n## Required implementation planning\\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-573c6141/events.jsonl\",\"id\":\"ddx-573c6141\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"helix\",\"phase:planning\",\"kind:architecture\",\"area:agent\",\"area:api\",\"area:ui\",\"area:docs\"],\"notes\":\"Closed as accidental duplicate created during tracker-context bug reproduction; No schema, resolver, UI, or e2e test changes are present to evaluate against any of the 6 acceptance criteria.\",\"priority\":2,\"session_id\":\"eb-c04f9f9f\",\"spec-id\":\"FEAT-008\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"artifact regeneration: artifactRegenerate mutation + UI action + artifacts.spec.ts e2e\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-05-01T07:29:34.996722045Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:322:{\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-30T03:39:42.445334383Z\",\"description\":\"Finding from fresh-eyes review of DDx-owned retry semantics.\\n\\nProblem:\\nThe current DDx plan correctly says DDx owns success classification and retry, but it does not define a hard eligibility matrix for when a failed run may be retried with higher MinPower. The upstream agent contract distinguishes capability/semantic failures from deterministic setup failures and says dirty worktrees, merge conflicts, invalid bead metadata, unresolved dependencies, config parse errors, missing binaries, auth failures, and command-not-found cases are not eligible for power retry.\\n\\nWhy this matters:\\nWithout an explicit matrix, DDx can burn stronger models on failures where model power is irrelevant. That keeps DDx involved in agent execution behavior in an ad hoc way and makes retry outcomes impossible to audit.\\n\\nRequired fix:\\nExtend FEAT-010 and ddx-9f82f325 with a retry eligibility table. DDx may escalate MinPower only for classified outcomes where more capable reasoning could plausibly change the result, using agent status/output as evidence but not inspecting provider/model routing details. Deterministic environment, tracker, dependency, config, auth, missing-binary, and passthrough-unsatisfied failures must stop as blocked/operator-action-required.\\n\\nAcceptance:\\n- Retry policy has named eligible and ineligible status classes.\\n- Tests cover at least one capability failure that escalates MinPower and several deterministic failures that do not.\\n- Audit records include the classification reason used for retry/stop.\\n- The policy never branches on provider/model identity except for passthrough constraint exhaustion reported by the agent as a typed status.\",\"id\":\"ddx-6232daf5\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"helix\",\"phase:build\",\"review-finding\",\"area:agent\",\"area:execution\"],\"parent\":\"ddx-deb9be7e\",\"priority\":1,\"spec_id\":\"review:power-passthrough-boundary-retry-eligibility\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"review finding: define retry eligibility before escalating agent power\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-30T03:50:03.4810409Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:335:{\"acceptance\":\"1. ddx artifact --help lists an artifact namespace. 2. ddx artifact regenerate ID exists and follows FEAT-010: layer-1 run for pure-return generators, layer-2 when the generator edits the repo, setting produces_artifact. 3. Missing generator/provenance returns a clear non-zero error. 4. Tests cover command registration and at least one regenerate error path. 5. The V6 visual-suite bead can invoke the command instead of using a manual fallback.\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-30T13:34:53Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"sindri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"4043997\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"b3bd5835ae12b4a1533363c33d46e3e07a161a46\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-30T13:31:51.302384407Z\",\"description\":\"V6 visual generation is hard-blocked on the artifact-regenerate implementation. No number on the page conflates those three categories.\\n\\n**Acceptance criteria:**\\n\\n1. **`billingMode` field.** Session records (index rows + live writer) carry `billingMode` ∈ `{paid, subscription, local}`. Enum is validated on write. Unit test covers each value.\\n\\n2. **Classifier.** `billingModeFor(harness, surface, baseURL)` is the single source of truth. Test table includes at minimum:\\n   - claude (Claude Code) → subscription\\n   - codex → subscription\\n   - openrouter (any model) → paid\\n   - openai/anthropic with API key → paid\\n   - agent (embedded) → local\\n   - openai-compat endpoint pointing at localhost/127.*/192.168.*/10.* → local\\n   - openai-compat endpoint pointing at a public host with an API key → paid\\n\\n3. **Reindex backfill.** Running `ddx agent log reindex` (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) on a fixture with sessions from all three modes produces index rows with correct `billingMode`. No row is written without one.\\n\\n4. **Rollup resolver.** GraphQL exposes `sessionsCostSummary(projectId, since?, until?) { cashUsd, subscriptionEquivUsd, localSessionCount, localEstimatedUsd }`. Returns zeros/null correctly when no sessions match. Respects shard-scoped reads from `ddx-0a33bc5f`.\\n\\n5. **UI — three cards.** The Sessions page replaces the \\\"Total Cost\\\" card with three cards as described (Cash paid, Subscription value, Local sessions). Tooltips explain each bucket. Playwright asserts all three render and that hover on each shows the explanatory text.\\n\\n6. **UI — per-row badge.** Each session row's cost column shows a small `cash`/`sub`/`local` badge with a tooltip explaining the bucket. Playwright asserts badge presence and correct classification on a seeded fixture containing one session of each type.\\n\\n7. **Zero-state.** When the project has zero sessions of a bucket, that card shows `—` / `0`, not `$0.00`, to avoid implying a paid-zero vs absent-data collision. Playwright asserts.\\n\\n8. **Optional local cost config.** Setting `cost.local_per_1k_tokens` in `.ddx/config.yaml` causes the Local card to render as dollars using the configured rate × sum(totalTokens) for local sessions. Unset: count-only. Integration test covers both paths.\\n\\n9. **Accessibility.** The three cards and per-row badges expose their bucket meaning via `aria-label`, not just tooltip, so screen-reader users get the same clarification.\\n\\n10. **Cross-references.**\\n    - Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) — the `billingMode` field must land in the index schema.\\n    - Bead notes in `ddx-23978824` should reference this bead since the unified-endpoints view has overlapping concepts (what's a paid endpoint vs a subscription harness vs a local endpoint).\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"51bb2f5d3c0de00361638822249616bcc5e3e615\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-22T20:54:25.174982037Z\",\"description\":\"## Observed\\n\\nThe Sessions page ($ sums `session.cost` into one \\\"Total Cost\\\" card, `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/.../sessions/+page.svelte:21,28,91`). The underlying `cost_usd` field is fed from three very different billing realities and the UI treats them as one:\\n\\n1. **Cash paid (pay-per-token).** API providers (OpenRouter, direct OpenAI, direct Anthropic API). `cost_usd` = actual billed amount.\\n2. **Subscription-equivalent.** Claude Code, Codex — flat-rate subscriptions. The CLI/harness emits `total_cost_usd` as the **dollar-equivalent** for tokens consumed under the subscription (what you would have paid pay-per-token). You paid a flat fee up front; this is a value-extracted number, not a cash-out number.\\n3. **Local compute.** Embedded `agent` harness, ollama/vllm endpoints. Today emits cost 0 because no one is billing; real cost is electricity + hardware amortization, not currently modeled.\\n\\nA user reading \\\"Total Cost: $X\\\" does not know which of those X is.\\n\\n## What's needed\\n\\nThree independent rollups, each explicitly labeled, each computable without ambiguity:\\n\\n1. **Cash** — sum of `cost_usd` across sessions whose billing mode is `paid`.\\n2. **Subscription-equivalent** — sum across sessions whose billing mode is `subscription`. Copy: \\\"Dollar-equivalent for tokens consumed via Claude Code / Codex subscription. You did not pay this in cash; it's the pay-per-token rate you would have been charged.\\\"\\n3. **Local** — either a count of sessions (if no rate is configured) or a computed dollar estimate if `local_cost_per_1k_tokens` is set in config. Default: count-only, to avoid inventing numbers.\\n\\n## Backing data model\\n\\nSession records (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) need a new field `billingMode: \\\"paid\\\" | \\\"subscription\\\" | \\\"local\\\"`. Derive at write time:\\n\\n- `paid`: harness is an API-endpoint provider (openrouter, openai-compat with an API key, anthropic API, …). Probably any harness with `Surface == \\\"openai-compat\\\"` or a known public-api provider prefix.\\n- `subscription`: harness is `claude` (Claude Code) or `codex` or `gemini-cli` when using a Google account. Identify by harness name + surface.\\n- `local`: harness is `agent` (embedded) OR endpoint is localhost / private IP / configured-as-local in provider config.\\n\\nClassification lives in one place — a small `billingModeFor(harness, surface, baseURL)` function — so the logic isn't duplicated between writer and reader.\\n\\n## Scope\\n\\n### Part 1 — Data model\\n\\n- Extend `SessionEntry` / the session index row with `billingMode string` (enum validated on write).\\n- Add `billingModeFor(...)` helper + unit tests for each harness/surface combination.\\n- Back-fill: `ddx agent log reindex` (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) computes `billingMode` when re-shard-writing legacy sessions.\\n\\n### Part 2 — Backend rollup\\n\\n- Modify the sessions GraphQL query (or add a sibling `sessionsCostSummary(projectId, since?)`) to return `{ cashUsd, subscriptionEquivUsd, localSessionCount, localEstimatedUsd? }`.\\n- Reuse the perf-safe path from `ddx-0a33bc5f` (sessions-index aggregation, shard-aware date filter).\\n\\n### Part 3 — UI\\n\\n- Replace the single \\\"Total Cost\\\" card on the Sessions page with three cards:\\n  - **Cash paid** ($X.XX) — tooltip \\\"Billed by pay-per-token APIs (OpenRouter, direct API keys)\\\"\\n  - **Subscription value** ($X.XX) — tooltip \\\"Dollar-equivalent for tokens consumed under Claude Code / Codex subscriptions. Not cash out of pocket.\\\"\\n  - **Local sessions** (N) — tooltip \\\"Sessions served locally. Compute cost not modeled.\\\" If `local_cost_per_1k_tokens` is set, show \\\"$X.XX est.\\\" instead of a count.\\n- Per-row: small badge `cash` / `sub` / `local` in the cost column, color-coded (green/blue/gray). Tooltip explains the row's bucket.\\n\\n### Part 4 — Optional config\\n\\n- `.ddx/config.yaml` gains `cost.local_per_1k_tokens: \\u003cfloat\\u003e`. When set, local-session total gets rendered as dollars. When unset, count-only. No auto-estimation.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Subscription flat-rate cost modeling (\\\"I pay $20/month for Claude Code\\\"). A useful adjacent bead: divide flat rate by consumption to show $/1M-tokens realized value. Not here.\\n- Quota trend / headroom. Covered by `ddx-23978824`.\\n- Reclassifying historical sessions beyond what reindex can infer from their stored harness/surface fields.\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-6904a90b/events.jsonl\",\"execute-loop-last-detail\":\"escalation exhausted: unsupported model \\\"minimax/minimax-m2.7\\\" for harness \\\"gemini\\\"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite\",\"execute-loop-last-status\":\"execution_failed\",\"execute-loop-no-changes-count\":1,\"feature\":\"FEAT-008\",\"id\":\"ddx-6904a90b\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"feat-008\",\"feat-010\",\"cost\",\"billing\",\"clarity\"],\"notes\":\"`ddx-23978824` bead notes are missing the required reference to `ddx-6904a90b`; `ddx bead show ddx-23978824` contains no `ddx-6904a90b`, and the implementation diff has no `.ddx/beads.jsonl` tracker update for that cross-reference.\",\"priority\":2,\"session_id\":\"eb-90c76cfa\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"Sessions 'Total cost' is ambiguous: split into cash paid, subscription-equivalent, and local compute\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-23T05:38:12.428163921Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:355:{\"acceptance\":\"**User story:** As an operator looking at the DDx web UI, I want to understand at a glance what Workers are vs Sessions, and I want to stop a misbehaving worker or start a new queue drain without dropping to a terminal, so the UI is a real operator surface instead of a read-only status page.\\n\\n**Acceptance criteria:**\\n\\n1. **Copy + IA.** Workers page header includes a one-sentence description distinguishing it from Sessions. Sessions page likewise. Each links to the other. Playwright asserts both descriptions are present and the cross-links navigate.\\n\\n2. **Worker → Sessions navigation.** On worker detail, a \\\"Sessions\\\" section lists sessions produced by that worker (join on `session.workerId`). Empty state reads \\\"No sessions recorded yet.\\\" If session records do not yet carry a worker ID, that gap is filled as part of this bead and backfilled for existing sessions in the current run (no historical backfill required).\\n\\n3. **Session → Worker backlink.** Expanded session row shows worker ID as a clickable link when present.\\n\\n4. **Stop worker.** Each row with `state == 'running'` shows a Stop button. Clicking prompts for confirm, then fires a GraphQL mutation that stops the worker. UI updates within 2s via the existing subscription/poll path. Integration test asserts: button visibility is gated on state, confirm dialog is required, and mutation invokes the same code path as `ddx agent workers stop`.\\n\\n5. **Start worker.** A \\\"Start worker\\\" button in the Workers page header opens a form (harness/profile, effort, optional label filter). Submitting fires a `startWorker` mutation. On success the new worker appears in the list within 2s. Form validation prevents empty submissions. The code path reuses `ddx work` / execute-loop internals — not a separate implementation.\\n\\n6. **Audit trail.** Every lifecycle action (start, stop, cancel) is recorded as a timestamped event visible on the worker detail page, including the actor identifier the server has for the requester.\\n\\n7. **No regressions.** Existing live log streaming, recent events, and phase overrides continue to work. Playwright smoke of the worker detail page passes.\\n\\n8. **Playwright e2e.**\\n   - Navigates to Workers page, asserts header description.\\n   - Clicks \\\"Start worker\\\", fills form, submits, asserts new row appears.\\n   - Clicks Stop on that row, confirms, asserts row state transitions to `stopped`.\\n   - Clicks into the stopped worker, asserts the Sessions section renders (or the empty state).\\n   - Asserts the \\\"Sessions\\\" link in the worker page header navigates to the Sessions page.\\n\\n9. **Design review sign-off.** Before implementation begins, a short design note (one page or less) in `docs/helix/02-design/` captures the Workers-vs-Sessions mental model, the chosen controls, and any CLI primitives that need to exist. Implementation PR links to that note.\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"1811cb188c77a7a20b042f8d08d579228d9368f4\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-22T20:18:04.067488813Z\",\"description\":\"## Observed\\n\\n`/nodes/.../projects/.../workers` renders a table of workers but has no buttons, no controls, and no explanation of how it differs from `/sessions`. The layout (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte`) shows ID / Kind / State / Current Bead / Attempts — read-only. Clicking a row drills into live-streaming logs and recent events, but offers no actions there either.\\n\\nThis raised a fair question: **what is this screen for, and how is it different from Sessions?** The answer isn't in the UI.\\n\\n## Ground truth (from the CLI)\\n\\n- **Worker** = a persistent execute-loop process draining the bead queue (`ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop`). Has a lifecycle: running / idle / stopped / error. Has a *current bead* and cumulative attempts/successes/failures. Can be stopped via `ddx agent workers stop`. Long-lived.\\n- **Session** = a historical record of one agent invocation (harness, model, bead, tokens, cost, outcome, start/end). Immutable after completion. One worker produces many sessions over its lifetime.\\n\\nSo: Workers are *processes you operate*. Sessions are *events you review*. The current UI exposes both as flat tables and lets the reader guess which is which.\\n\\n## Gaps\\n\\n1. **No disambiguation.** No page-level copy, no link between the two, no worker→sessions navigation (\\\"show sessions produced by this worker\\\"), no session→worker backlink.\\n2. **No lifecycle controls on Workers.** The CLI has `ddx agent workers stop` and `ddx work` (start). The UI has neither button. No stop, start, restart, or \\\"cancel current bead\\\" affordance. The State column is a spectator sport.\\n3. **No worker start UX.** There is no way to start a queue drain from the UI — users must drop to a terminal.\\n4. **Unclear empty state.** \\\"No workers found.\\\" does not say whether that is normal (nothing running) or broken (server can't see workers). No hint about how to start one.\\n5. **No worker details beyond logs.** Worker detail page shows live phase + log tail + tool calls but not: worker config (project path, profile/harness, started-at, last-heartbeat), resource usage, or the list of sessions this worker has produced.\\n\\n## Proposed direction\\n\\nSplit into two deliverables — do them together if we're touching this screen anyway, but they are independently landable:\\n\\n### A. Information architecture (copy + navigation)\\n\\n- Add a short header description to each page: \\\"Workers drain the bead queue. Sessions are the history of what they ran.\\\"\\n- Mutual links: Workers page links to \\\"Recent sessions →\\\", Sessions page links to \\\"Workers →\\\".\\n- On worker detail: a \\\"Sessions\\\" tab or section listing sessions where `session.workerId == this.id` (requires session records to carry workerID; check whether they do).\\n- On session detail (expandable row already exists): if the session has a workerID, surface it as a link back to the worker.\\n\\n### B. Lifecycle controls\\n\\n- **Stop** button per running worker row → calls a GraphQL mutation that invokes the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop \\u003cid\\u003e`. Confirms before acting.\\n- **Start worker** button in the page header → opens a small form (project, profile/harness, effort, optional bead filter) and calls a `startWorker` mutation that shells out to `ddx work` in the background. The form should default to sane values and be submittable in one click for the common case.\\n- **Cancel current bead** action on the worker detail page — signals the worker to abandon the in-flight bead without killing the loop. (Only if CLI primitive exists; otherwise descope and note in the bead.)\\n- All mutations must be idempotent and log an audit record viewable in the UI (who did it, when). Reuse existing session-event plumbing if possible.\\n\\n### Out of scope\\n\\n- Rewriting execute-loop semantics.\\n- Cross-project worker orchestration.\\n- Auth / permissions model for who can stop workers (assume single-operator for now; file a follow-up if multi-operator becomes real).\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-69789664/events.jsonl\",\"execute-loop-last-detail\":\"escalation exhausted: unsupported model \\\"minimax/minimax-m2.7\\\" for harness \\\"gemini\\\"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite\",\"execute-loop-last-status\":\"execution_failed\",\"execute-loop-no-changes-count\":1,\"feature\":\"FEAT-008\",\"id\":\"ddx-69789664\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"feat-008\",\"feat-010\",\"feat-006\",\"ui\",\"design\"],\"notes\":\"Prerequisite: ddx-05b4cc9d fixes the project-scoped Workers resolver so workersByProject(projectID:) returns workers for the selected project. User feedback: they're not sure what to do with it. It does not match their mental model of what efficacy is (a session aggregation grouped by provider / harness / model).\\n\\n## Ground truth\\n\\n- The backing feature is `Query.comparisons` and the comparison record set (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:211-226`). It's an A/B-style \\\"run the same prompt across N arms and compare results\\\" tool. That is a real capability — just not what a rollup/efficacy dashboard is for.\\n- It's colocated with Efficacy today probably because both involve harness/model performance. The colocation is creating confusion: the Efficacy page promises \\\"how are things doing\\\" and the Compare button promises \\\"design an experiment\\\" — different audiences, different tasks.\\n\\n## Decision required\\n\\nBefore implementation, decide one of three:\\n\\n1. **Relocate.** Move Compare to its own top-level surface (e.g., `/experiments` or `/compare`) and cleanly separate \\\"how did things go\\\" (efficacy) from \\\"design an A/B\\\" (compare). Efficacy page loses the Compare button entirely.\\n2. **Redesign in place.** Keep it on the Efficacy page but recast as \\\"Compare these rows\\\" — the user selects 2+ efficacy rows and launches a comparison pre-seeded from them. Removes the cold-start prompt-entry UX; connects directly to the rollup the user is already looking at.\\n3. **Remove.** If comparisons are not being used by real workflows, drop the feature and the associated UI/resolver. Least code, least surface area.\\n\\nThe user did not mandate a specific choice — they flagged the confusion and the mental model. Option 2 aligns best with their stated mental model (efficacy = session aggregation); option 1 is the cleanest separation of concerns; option 3 is viable only if usage data supports it.\\n\\n## Proposed direction\\n\\nRun the design decision as a short spike (≤ half a day):\\n\\n- Check whether `comparisons` is referenced by anything beyond this UI page: CLI, docs, specs, other server endpoints. The bead-id span in `BeadDetail.svelte` uses `truncate min-w-0` (or equivalent) instead of `shrink-0`. The parent flex container keeps `min-w-0`.\\n2. The span has `title={bead.id}` so hovering reveals the full value.\\n3. A copy-to-clipboard icon button sits immediately after the id. Clicking copies the full id to the clipboard and surfaces a subtle confirmation (existing toast / inline check mark — whichever matches project conventions).\\n4. Action buttons (Claim/Unclaim, Edit, Delete, Close) remain fully visible and clickable regardless of id length. Status pill and owner chip remain visible; they may wrap below the id on narrow viewports but never overlap the action group.\\n5. Playwright e2e:\\n   - Seeds a bead with a 60-char id (e.g. `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-526efaf1`) and opens its detail panel.\\n   - Asserts the id element has a truncation class and that its rendered width is ≤ the parent container width minus the action-button group width.\\n   - Asserts the Delete button is clickable (`.click()` does not throw) and that its bounding rect does not intersect the id span's bounding rect.\\n   - Asserts hovering the id shows a tooltip (or reveals `title` attribute) with the full value, and clicking the copy button writes the full id to the clipboard.\\n6. Unit/visual: at `max-w-xl` width the header renders without horizontal scroll and without clipped action buttons for ids of length 8, 32, 60, and 120.\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-24T06:44:49Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"eitri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"196235\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"a3a9887a4a5b098e2e986dff13d3b514c7b476f5\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-22T20:52:31.873838627Z\",\"description\":\"## Observed\\n\\nOn the bead detail panel, a long bead id overflows and visually overlaps the Claim/Unclaim/Edit/Delete/Close buttons on the right. Reporter example: `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-526efaf1` (~60 chars).\\n\\n## Root cause\\n\\n`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/BeadDetail.svelte:211-221` —\\n\\n```svelte\\n\\u003cdiv class=\\\"flex shrink-0 items-center justify-between border-b ... px-6 py-4\\\"\\u003e\\n  \\u003cdiv class=\\\"flex min-w-0 items-center gap-3\\\"\\u003e\\n    \\u003cspan class=\\\"shrink-0 font-mono text-xs ...\\\"\\u003e{bead.id}\\u003c/span\\u003e       ← shrink-0 prevents truncation\\n    \\u003cspan class=\\\"shrink-0 font-medium ...\\\"\\u003e{bead.status}\\u003c/span\\u003e\\n    {#if bead.owner}\\n      \\u003cspan class=\\\"truncate text-xs ...\\\"\\u003e@ {bead.owner}\\u003c/span\\u003e\\n    {/if}\\n  \\u003c/div\\u003e\\n  \\u003cdiv class=\\\"ml-3 flex shrink-0 items-center gap-2\\\"\\u003e\\n    [Claim/Unclaim] [Edit] [Delete] [Close]\\n  \\u003c/div\\u003e\\n\\u003c/div\\u003e\\n```\\n\\nThe bead-id span has `shrink-0`, so it never truncates regardless of length. The outer panel is `max-w-xl` (576px). A 60-char monospace id occupies roughly 400-500px on its own; combined with the status pill, owner chip, and the action-button group (also `shrink-0`), it pushes past the available width. Flex layout collapses by overlapping rather than wrapping, so the id draws on top of the buttons.\\n\\n## Proposed direction\\n\\n1. **Replace `shrink-0` on the bead-id span with `truncate min-w-0`.** The parent `\\u003cdiv class=\\\"flex min-w-0 items-center gap-3\\\"\\u003e` already establishes a shrinkable container.\\n2. **Add `title={bead.id}` so hovering shows the full value** (important — truncating monospace ids from the right loses the distinguishing suffix).\\n3. **Add a copy-to-clipboard icon button** next to the id so operators can grab the full value with one click when the id is truncated. Small icon button, no extra visual weight.\\n4. **Sanity check:** the reporter's example starts with `.` and has the shape of an execute-bead worktree name. Worth briefly checking whether something is producing such a value as a bead id (worktree name leaking into bead creation?) — but that's a separate investigation. The UI still needs to degrade gracefully regardless.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Changing bead id generation or enforcing max length on ids.\\n- Redesign of the detail-panel header layout.\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-71fbe968/events.jsonl\",\"execute-loop-heartbeat-at\":\"2026-04-24T06:48:19.884413094Z\",\"execute-loop-last-detail\":\"escalation exhausted: unsupported model \\\"minimax/minimax-m2.7\\\" for harness \\\"gemini\\\"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite\",\"execute-loop-last-status\":\"execution_failed\",\"feature\":\"FEAT-008\",\"id\":\"ddx-71fbe968\",\"issue_type\":\"bug\",\"labels\":[\"feat-008\",\"feat-004\",\"ui\",\"css\"],\"owner\":\"ddx\",\"priority\":3,\"session_id\":\"eb-78023e02\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"Bead detail header: long bead ids overlap action buttons (shrink-0 prevents truncation)\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-24T06:48:58.508228919Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:383:{\"acceptance\":\"**User story:** As a developer reviewing what my agents have done, I open the Executions view, filter to a specific bead or verdict or harness, click into one, and see the prompt it received, the verdict it produced, the session it corresponds to (with cost + tokens), and every tool call it made — all without leaving the browser.\\n\\n**Acceptance criteria:**\\n\\n1. **Sidebar entry.** \\\"Executions\\\" added to the project sidebar between \\\"Sessions\\\" and \\\"Personas\\\" (or wherever fits the existing visual order — design call; captured in the PR description). Active-highlight works.\\n\\n2. **List route.** `/executions` renders a paginated, filterable table with columns: timestamp, bead (id + title as link), harness/model, verdict (color-coded pass/block/error/other), duration, cost. Default sort: newest first. Filters: verdict, harness, bead, since/until date, free-text search over prompt first ~200 chars.\\n\\n3. **Detail route.** `/executions/\\u003cid\\u003e` shows five tabs as described (Manifest, Prompt, Result, Session, Tool calls). Tab switching doesn't re-fetch already-loaded data.\\n\\n4. **Tool calls pane.** Virtualized list. First 50 calls load with the page; subsequent pages load on scroll. Each entry shows tool name + collapsed input/output, expandable on click. Links to the raw agent-log file for operators who want to diff.\\n\\n5. **GraphQL completeness.** `executions`, `execution`, `executionToolCalls` resolvers exist and pass feat-008-style integration tests. Schema documented in `schema.graphql`.\\n\\n6. **Cross-links present.**\\n   - Bead detail renders an \\\"Executions\\\" section listing linked executions with verdict + timestamp, each clicking into the detail route.\\n   - Session detail shows \\\"Execution\\\" link when the session has one.\\n   - Commits page shows execution link when commit sha == execution.result_rev.\\n\\n7. **Fixture + perf.**\\n   - Seeded fixture: ≥1,000 executions, ≥100 with seeded tool-call streams (≥50 calls each).\\n   - List view on this fixture: p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP.\\n   - Detail view (no tool calls): p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP.\\n   - Tool-call page 1 (first 50 entries): p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.\\n   - If any target cannot be met in-place, raise DB-substrate decision with user before switching.\\n\\n8. **Playwright e2e.**\\n   - Seeds a project with 3 executions across 2 beads with mixed verdicts.\\n   - Navigates to `/executions`, asserts the table renders 3 rows and filter-by-verdict narrows correctly.\\n   - Clicks into one execution, asserts all 5 tabs exist and the Prompt tab shows the seeded prompt.\\n   - Opens the Tool calls tab, expands an entry, asserts input/output rendering.\\n   - Clicks the linked bead from the detail, asserts navigation to bead detail and that the Executions section on that bead lists this execution.\\n\\n9. **Depends on.**\\n   - Soft dependency on `ddx-2ceb02fa` for the Session tab's cost/token numbers. If not yet landed, render \\\"not available\\\" for those fields and wire them in after.\\n   - Adjacent: `ddx-6904a90b` (billing mode) — Session tab's cost shows the correct bucket once that lands.\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"59a4ece5923fa29a89addac195937780c28ec955\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-22T20:55:34.427486388Z\",\"description\":\"## Observed / Motivation\\n\\n`.ddx/executions/\\u003cts\\u003e-\\u003chash\\u003e/{manifest.json, prompt.md, result.json}` bundles are already being produced per `ddx agent execute-bead` attempt — 469 of them on the current repo. They contain the canonical record of what an agent was asked to do and what verdict the attempt produced. **Nothing in the web UI surfaces them today.** A user who wants to ask \\\"why did this bead end up in this state?\\\" has to drop to the filesystem.\\n\\nSessions (`sessions.jsonl` / sharded index per `ddx-2ceb02fa`) carry the harness/cost/token telemetry but not the verdict and not the bead linkage. Agent log streams (`agent-*.jsonl`) carry the tool-call trace but are scattered across hundreds of files. **Executions are the natural join** — they connect a bead, a session, a prompt, a verdict, and a tool-call stream.\\n\\n## Ground truth\\n\\nPer-execution on disk:\\n\\n```\\n.ddx/executions/\\u003cts\\u003e-\\u003chash\\u003e/\\n  manifest.json      { harness, model, base_rev, result_rev, verdict, bead_id, execution_dir }\\n  prompt.md          the prompt that was sent\\n  result.json        { verdict, rationale, ... }\\n```\\n\\nRelations:\\n- `manifest.bead_id` → bead\\n- a session id is recorded in bead events (`kind:cost`, `kind:routing`) and in the agent-log stream; both can map back to an execution via correlation metadata\\n- tool calls live in `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` (and `agent-s-*.jsonl`), keyed by session id\\n\\n## Scope\\n\\n### Part 1 — GraphQL surface\\n\\n- `executions(projectId, first, after, beadId?, since?, until?, verdict?, harness?)` — paginated list of execution bundles.\\n- `execution(id)` — one bundle, fully materialized: manifest fields, prompt markdown, result JSON, linked bead (id + title), linked session (id + harness + model + cost + token totals), tool-call stream pointer.\\n- `executionToolCalls(id, first, after)` — paginated tool-call stream for the execution, read lazily from the agent-log file. Matches the event shape used by the workers detail page.\\n\\nExecutions source of truth: directory scan of `.ddx/executions/` filtered/paginated at the resolver. Shard discipline: follow the sessions-index monthly shard pattern (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) if listing all executions grows beyond tens of thousands — per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`, raise the storage decision with the user before switching substrate.\\n\\n### Part 2 — Sidebar + routes\\n\\n- Add \\\"Executions\\\" to the project sidebar (`NavShell.svelte`).\\n- Routes:\\n  - `/nodes/\\u003cn\\u003e/projects/\\u003cp\\u003e/executions` — list view with filters (verdict, harness, bead, date range, free-text over prompt). Table columns: timestamp, bead, harness/model, verdict (pass/block/error), duration, cost.\\n  - `/nodes/\\u003cn\\u003e/projects/\\u003cp\\u003e/executions/\\u003cid\\u003e` — detail view with five tabs: **Manifest**, **Prompt** (rendered markdown), **Result** (rationale + raw JSON toggle), **Session** (harness, model, cost, token totals, link to `/sessions/\\u003cid\\u003e`), **Tool calls** (virtualized lazy-scroll list of tool_use + tool_result pairs with expandable payloads).\\n\\n### Part 3 — Cross-links (make the graph real)\\n\\n- **Bead detail:** new \\\"Executions\\\" section listing all executions for this bead with verdict + timestamp + link. Already have data: manifest.bead_id.\\n- **Session detail:** new \\\"Execution\\\" link pointing to the execution this session ran for, when the session has a linked execution.\\n- **Commits page:** already shows commits; if a commit was produced by an execution (result_rev == commit sha), surface the execution link.\\n- **Graph/Docs pages:** out of scope for this bead.\\n\\n### Part 4 — Perf\\n\\n- Fixture: ≥1,000 executions, per standing directive `ddx-9ce6842a`. Tool-call streams seeded for 10% of them.\\n- Targets: list view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP, detail view without tool calls p95 ≤ 150ms HTTP, tool-call page-1 (first 50) p95 ≤ 300ms HTTP.\\n- If these can't be met with filesystem scan + in-memory filter, raise DB-substrate decision with user per standing directive.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Writing new execution bundles (that's already happening).\\n- Executions from other projects/nodes (keep per-project for v1).\\n- Tool-call payload redaction / privacy. File a follow-up if relevant.\\n- `.claude/worktrees/` worktree lifecycle tracking — covered by companion bead per user's split request.\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-726bc5f2/events.jsonl\",\"execute-loop-last-detail\":\"escalation exhausted: unsupported model \\\"minimax/minimax-m2.7\\\" for harness \\\"gemini\\\"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite\",\"execute-loop-last-status\":\"execution_failed\",\"execute-loop-no-changes-count\":1,\"feature\":\"FEAT-008\",\"id\":\"ddx-726bc5f2\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"feat-008\",\"feat-010\",\"feat-006\",\"ui\",\"executions\"],\"notes\":\"REVIEW:BLOCK\\n\\n**All AC items (1–9):** The diff adds `.ddx/executions/20260423T053812-719b9c5f/result.json`, which is execution metadata from the agent run that attempted this bead. Commit light+dark screenshot baselines.\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-794b7a72/events.jsonl\",\"execute-loop-heartbeat-at\":\"2026-04-22T08:58:34.344352326Z\",\"id\":\"ddx-794b7a72\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"ui\",\"feat-008\",\"phase-8\",\"us-097\"],\"owner\":\"ddx\",\"parent\":\"ddx-4a9d30db\",\"priority\":1,\"session_id\":\"eb-381fd9f3\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"FEAT-008 P8: US-097 personas cards + bind flow (REWRITE existing page)\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-22T09:02:39.416018026Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:411:{\"acceptance\":\"**User story:** As an operator, I can see every execute-bead worktree on my project, understand which ones are active vs completed vs abandoned, and clean up abandoned ones with confirmation without shelling out to git commands.\\n\\n**Acceptance criteria:**\\n\\n1. **Inventory resolver.** `executeBeadWorktrees(projectId)` returns structured entries as described. Unit test covers: (a) active worktree with live worker, (b) completed worktree with matching execution bundle and head==result_rev, (c) abandoned worktree past the threshold, (d) orphan (path without git registration).\\n\\n2. **State classification.** `state` is computed deterministically from inputs (workers list, execution bundles, git worktree list, filesystem stat, configured threshold). Classifier is a single pure function with table-driven tests covering all four states and at least two edge cases per state.\\n\\n3. **UI — list view.** `/worktrees` route renders a table with columns path/branch/bead/execution/state/created/last-activity/disk. Sortable by any column. Filters: state, bead. Default sort: newest `last activity` first.\\n\\n4. **UI — actions.** Per-row: Remove (confirm-gated), Force-remove (for orphans, extra-confirm-gated with typed confirmation of the path). Both actions call through to git + filesystem via a mutation; neither is performed client-side.\\n\\n5. **CLI — prune.** `ddx server state worktree-prune [--dry-run] [--project \\u003cpath\\u003e]` lists abandoned+orphan worktrees and prompts for removal (unless `--yes`). Dry-run prints the list without touching anything. Integration test runs against a fixture repo with one of each state and asserts only the expected ones are targeted.\\n\\n6. **Sweep + notice.** Server background sweep runs at a configurable interval (default 15m), logs a summary, and exposes a GraphQL counter (`executeBeadWorktreeCounters { total, active, abandoned, orphan }`) for dashboard rollup. Sweep never deletes unless `worktrees.auto_prune: true` is set in config.\\n\\n7. **Cross-links.**\\n   - From a worktree row, click execution → navigates to `/executions/\\u003cid\\u003e` (depends on `ddx-726bc5f2`).\\n   - From an execution detail page, a \\\"Worktree\\\" section shows the worktree (if still present) with the same state badge.\\n\\n8. **Fixture + perf.** Per standing directive in `ddx-9ce6842a`, seed a fixture with ≥100 worktrees (mix of states). List view p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP. This is small-n territory; should be well within filesystem-scan limits, no DB substrate expected.\\n\\n9. **Playwright e2e.**\\n   - Seeds fixture with one worktree of each of the four states.\\n   - Navigates to `/worktrees`, asserts all four rows with correct state badges.\\n   - Clicks Remove on an abandoned worktree, confirms, asserts the row disappears within 2s and the worktree is gone from `git worktree list`.\\n   - Attempts Remove on an active worktree, asserts the confirm dialog warns and the action is gated.\\n\\n10. **Path convention decision note.** Before Part 4 lands, a one-page note in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the canonical location choice and the migration plan. Parts 1-3 may ship without this note; Part 4 requires it.\\n\\n11. **Cross-references.** Notes link to: `ddx-12cae4dd` (documents noise), `ddx-71fbe968` (long id overlap — same worktree-name leak into UI), `ddx-726bc5f2` (executions UI — worktrees complement it), `ddx-15f7ee0b` (project registry cleanup pattern reused here).\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"6449424a64780692a3295a7a061db87d52f17eb8\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-22T20:56:37.613502322Z\",\"description\":\"## Observed\\n\\n`git worktree list` on the current ddx repo shows **39 worktrees**:\\n- 1 main worktree (the clone itself).\\n- ~37 under `.claude/worktrees/agent-\\u003chash\\u003e/` from execute-bead runs. Branch names `worktree-agent-\\u003chash\\u003e`.\\n- At least 1 under `/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-\\u003cbeadId\\u003e-\\u003cts\\u003e-\\u003chash\\u003e` (detached HEAD) — different path convention, different semantics.\\n\\nThere is no UI visibility into any of this. A user cannot see which worktrees exist, what bead each one belongs to, whether each is active/abandoned/completed, or clean them up short of `git worktree prune` + `rm -rf`. The `.claude/worktrees/` entries also leak into other surfaces (already tracked):\\n- Documents view noise (`ddx-12cae4dd`).\\n- One was surviving in the project registry cleanup earlier today (`proj-4c1ca90e: agent-a7bf6d44`).\\n\\n## Why the two path conventions?\\n\\n- `.claude/worktrees/agent-\\u003chash\\u003e` — created with a branch (`worktree-agent-\\u003chash\\u003e`), likely by interactive execute-bead runs or an earlier worktree convention.\\n- `/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-\\u003cbead\\u003e-\\u003cts\\u003e-\\u003chash\\u003e` — detached HEAD, under `/tmp`, convention that ships with current execute-bead (this is what's producing the long \\\"bead id\\\"-shaped names that leaked into UI in `ddx-71fbe968`).\\n\\nTwo conventions is itself a smell: inconsistent path discipline makes cleanup harder and makes cross-referencing an execution bundle → worktree unreliable.\\n\\n## What's needed\\n\\n### Part 1 — Inventory resolver\\n\\n- GraphQL: `executeBeadWorktrees(projectId)` → list of `{path, branch, beadId?, executionId?, baseRev, headRev, state, createdAt, lastActivityAt}`.\\n- Source: `git worktree list --porcelain` + filesystem stat + manifest lookup for executions whose `execution_dir` matches or whose path correlates.\\n- `state` ∈ `{active, completed, abandoned, orphan}`:\\n  - **active** — an execute-bead session is currently running against this worktree (cross-reference workers / sessions).\\n  - **completed** — associated execution bundle exists with a verdict; head matches `result_rev`.\\n  - **abandoned** — no recent activity (configurable, default 24h), no matching running worker, but worktree still present.\\n  - **orphan** — path on disk but `git worktree list` does not know about it (or vice versa).\\n\\n### Part 2 — Sidebar + route\\n\\n- New \\\"Worktrees\\\" view, or slot this into the Executions detail (`ddx-726bc5f2`) as a sidebar panel since every worktree is for some execution. Design call; recommend a dedicated view so orphans (which have no execution) are still visible.\\n- Route: `/nodes/\\u003cn\\u003e/projects/\\u003cp\\u003e/worktrees`.\\n- Columns: path, branch, bead (link), execution (link), state, created, last activity, disk usage.\\n- Row actions: **Open execution**, **Remove worktree** (confirm-gated; calls `git worktree remove` and cleans up `.claude/worktrees/\\u003cname\\u003e`), **Force remove** (for orphans; `-f`).\\n\\n### Part 3 — Policy + cleanup\\n\\n- Background sweep (server-side, already has a sweep pattern from project registry): every N minutes, list worktrees, detect abandoned state, emit a notice (but **never auto-delete** — user-confirmed cleanup only by default).\\n- `ddx server state worktree-prune [--dry-run]` CLI command that enumerates and optionally removes abandoned worktrees. Same deletion path as the UI action.\\n- Config: `.ddx/config.yaml: worktrees.abandoned_after: 24h`, `worktrees.auto_prune: false`. Opt-in auto-prune for teams that want it.\\n\\n### Part 4 — Path convention unification\\n\\n- Decide a single canonical location for execute-bead worktrees. Recommend `.claude/worktrees/\\u003cbead\\u003e-\\u003cts\\u003e-\\u003chash\\u003e/` or a sibling `.ddx/worktrees/` — **pick one and stick with it**.\\n- Migrate existing paths lazily: on first sweep after upgrade, record the path as-is and leave it; new worktrees use the new convention. Old ones phase out as they complete or get cleaned.\\n- This is a separable sub-effort; acceptable to land Parts 1-3 against the current mixed reality and do Part 4 afterward.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Changing how execute-bead creates worktrees (beyond the canonical-location choice in Part 4).\\n- Documents-view noise — covered by `ddx-12cae4dd`.\\n- Long-id display overlap — covered by `ddx-71fbe968`.\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-7aa810b8/events.jsonl\",\"execute-loop-last-detail\":\"escalation exhausted: unsupported model \\\"minimax/minimax-m2.7\\\" for harness \\\"gemini\\\"; Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, SD-012, API-001, the closed single-project loop design bead ddx-8bb9af7c, and the requirement to ship a tested single-project execution loop before multi-project or multi-host expansion.\\n\\u003c/context-digest\\u003e\\nImplement the first shipped single-project loop that continuously works one project's executable-bead queue using the explicit execute-bead contract.\\n\\n## Scope\\n- Monitor one project queue for execution-ready beads using the documented queue-ordering contract and the generic execution validator as a gate\\n- Run `ddx agent execute-bead` for the selected bead from the project root; do not introduce a second worktree lifecycle around the command\\n- Run required post-run validity checks and execution checks before classifying the attempt\\n- On execution failure, validation failure, or land conflict, preserve the iteration and evidence exactly as the execute-bead contract specifies, leave the target branch untouched, record the non-landed result, and continue scanning\\n- On success, land by rebase plus fast-forward, reset loop state to the updated branch tip, and continue scanning for the next executable bead\\n- Support a minimal control surface suitable for standalone use now and ddx-server hosting later without pulling in multi-project scheduling\\n\\n## Boundaries\\n- Single-project only for the first release\\n- No inline PTY policy or hidden HELIX scheduling heuristics\\n- Multi-project worker pools and multi-host control planes remain follow-on work\",\"id\":\"ddx-7bf45ce1\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"helix\",\"phase:build\",\"area:agent\",\"area:bead\",\"area:cli\",\"area:api\"],\"parent\":\"ddx-aac79489\",\"priority\":0,\"spec-id\":\"FEAT-006\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"implement: single-project execute-bead loop\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-10T17:08:15.039791148Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:418:{\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-30T12:28:53Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"sindri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"3902537\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"133dc5f47d89a8eaffdb1afd39605e6078c0b0d9\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-29T15:20:30.208351335Z\",\"dependencies\":[{\"issue_id\":\"ddx-7d4d2249\",\"depends_on_id\":\"ddx-751c661a\",\"type\":\"blocks\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-29T15:21:00Z\"},{\"issue_id\":\"ddx-7d4d2249\",\"depends_on_id\":\"ddx-90234d47\",\"type\":\"blocks\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-29T15:21:00Z\"}],\"description\":\"Per plan: docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md. BLOCKED ON #3 and #5/#6.\",\"execute-loop-heartbeat-at\":\"2026-04-30T12:28:53.997328454Z\",\"id\":\"ddx-7d4d2249\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"frame\",\"plan-2026-04-29\",\"agent-service\"],\"owner\":\"erik\",\"priority\":1,\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"[artifact-run-arch] update FEAT-006 (shrink to layer-1 consumer + passthrough + non-bead Profile)\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-30T12:29:44.406083598Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:419:{\"acceptance\":\"**User story:** As a developer on the plugins page, I want clicking Update or Install to give me continuous feedback until the operation completes, and I want the card to reflect the new installed state automatically — without a page reload.\\n\\n**Acceptance criteria:**\\n\\n1. **Per-card in-flight tracking.** Clicking Update/Install records the workerId under the plugin's name in a `Map\\u003cstring, string\\u003e`. The same plugin cannot be dispatched again while it is in flight (button disabled; tooltip shows the worker link). A different plugin can be dispatched concurrently and tracks its own worker.\\n\\n2. **Worker-driven refresh.** For each in-flight plugin, the page subscribes to that worker's progress via the existing `subscribeWorkerProgress` channel. On a terminal event, the page refetches the plugins list and clears that plugin's entry from the in-flight map. Within 2s of worker terminal event, the card reflects the new status (e.g., `update-available` → `installed`, new `installedVersion`).\\n\\n3. **Fallback polling.** If no terminal event arrives within 30s of dispatch, the page begins polling `plugins { name status installedVersion }` every 2s until the card's status or installedVersion changes, then stops. Integration test simulates a stream dropout and asserts the card eventually refreshes via the poll path.\\n\\n4. **In-flight visual state.** While a card is in flight, its status badge is replaced by a spinner + \\\"Updating…\\\" or \\\"Installing…\\\" text. The action button is disabled with a tooltip pointing to the worker.\\n\\n5. **Failure surfacing.** If the worker terminates in a failure phase, the card shows an error badge + tooltip \\\"Update failed — view worker\\\" linking to the worker page. The card's status/version remains whatever it was before dispatch (no false success). The global `dispatchError` stays for synchronous mutation errors only.\\n\\n6. **Concurrent dispatch.** Dispatching Update on two different cards at once shows two active worker links (a row or small stack in the header instead of the single slot), and each card tracks its own state. Playwright asserts.\\n\\n7. **No extra refetch overhead.** Refetch is scoped to the plugins list, not the full page data. Manual verification is enough; no specific perf test required.\\n\\n8. **Playwright e2e.**\\n   - Seeds a fixture with one outdated plugin.\\n   - Clicks Update.\\n   - Asserts the card enters the in-flight visual state and the action button is disabled.\\n   - Simulates the worker completing successfully.\\n   - Asserts within 2s the card shows `installed` and a newer `installedVersion`.\\n   - Runs a second scenario where the simulated worker fails; asserts the failure badge and that status stays `update-available`.\\n\\n9. **No regressions.** Install-from-registry flow (the ConfirmDialog path) adopts the same in-flight tracking; existing Playwright install flow still passes.\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"83b98c1c3e1c43e2e131635eef19f0861760439c\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-22T20:32:29.080474392Z\",\"description\":\"## Observed\\n\\nOn `/nodes/.../projects/.../plugins`, clicking Update on the outdated `helix` card:\\n\\n1. Fires the mutation — works.\\n2. Shows a link to the dispatched worker — helpful.\\n3. Worker completes and the plugin is actually updated on disk — verified.\\n4. **Card still shows `update-available`.** The UI never closes the loop.\\n\\nThe user has no in-UI signal that the update succeeded. They must reload the page (or navigate away and back) to see the new state. Worse, nothing prevents them from clicking the button again during the gap — firing a second update.\\n\\n## Root cause\\n\\n`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/plugins/+page.svelte:74-89` —\\n\\n```js\\nasync function dispatchPlugin(name, action, scope = 'project') {\\n  const result = await client.request(PLUGIN_DISPATCH_MUTATION, { name, action, scope });\\n  workerId = result.pluginDispatch.id;\\n}\\n```\\n\\nThat is the entire post-dispatch behavior. `data.plugins` is the snapshot from `+page.ts` at load time. There is no:\\n\\n- subscription to the dispatched worker's progress,\\n- polling of the plugin list,\\n- refetch on worker terminal event,\\n- optimistic card state while the worker runs,\\n- per-card in-flight tracking (only a single `workerId: string | null` at module scope, which means dispatching a second plugin overwrites the first worker link).\\n\\nThe workers layout already has `subscribeWorkerProgress(workerID, cb)` (`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte:22-30`) — the plugins page just doesn't use it.\\n\\n## Proposed direction\\n\\nTwo changes, smallest first:\\n\\n### Fix A — Close the loop on the dispatched worker.\\n\\n- Per-card in-flight tracking: replace the singleton `workerId` with a `Map\\u003cpluginName, workerId\\u003e`. Each card shows its own worker link + in-flight state; clicking Install/Update is disabled while that plugin is in flight.\\n- Subscribe to `subscribeWorkerProgress` for each in-flight plugin. On a terminal phase event (`completed`, `failed`, etc. — whatever the existing worker event vocabulary uses), refetch the plugins list and clear that plugin's in-flight entry.\\n- If the existing worker-progress stream doesn't emit a terminal event with enough detail, fall back to polling `plugins { name status installedVersion }` every 2s while any card is in flight, stopping when none are.\\n\\n### Fix B — Optimistic card state (optional polish).\\n\\n- While a card is in-flight, show \\\"Updating…\\\" / \\\"Installing…\\\" with a spinner in place of the status badge, and disable the action button. This is orthogonal to Fix A and can ship with it.\\n\\n### Fix C — Persistent failure surfacing.\\n\\n- Currently `dispatchError` is a single string. If the worker terminates with a failure phase, surface that per-card (error badge + tooltip with detail link to the worker page) rather than relying on `dispatchError` which only covers the synchronous mutation error.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Designing new worker terminal events. Use whatever the workers layout already consumes.\\n- Cancelling an in-flight plugin update — follow-up if needed.\\n- Bulk update (\\\"update all outdated\\\"). Its ID is referenced in this bead's events.\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-30T00:56:04Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"eitri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"2278885\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"e1e5f7cfd8c9656260461a7e63d3e67883bdd77a\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-29T19:05:33.7239674Z\",\"description\":\"## Two-layer problem\\n\\n### Layer 1 (mechanical bug)\\n\\nWhen a worker invokes `ddx bead create` from inside its execute-bead worktree, the resulting bead IDs are malformed. Reproduced today on this project:\\n\\n  /home/erik/.local/bin/ddx bead ready  → 11 entries with IDs like\\n  `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-44236615-20260429T185004-ffa9adf1-e630aeae`\\n\\nThese were the read-coverage child beads spawned from the ddx-44236615 worker (commit ebeb2464). They have valid descriptions, AC, priorities, and labels — only the ID field is mangled. The pattern `.execute-bead-wt-\\u003cparent\\u003e-\\u003ctimestamp\\u003e-\\u003cshort\\u003e-\\u003crandom\\u003e` matches the worktree directory name format used by `ddx agent execute-bead`. Most likely failure path: the bead-id resolver consults `os.Getwd()` (or equivalent) to derive a project context prefix, sees the worktree dir, and uses it instead of the real project root + a fresh `ddx-\\u003c8hex\\u003e` short hash.\\n\\nConcrete impact:\\n- 50+ char IDs are unwieldy in CLI commands (`ddx bead show \\u003cid\\u003e`, `ddx bead dep add ...`).\\n- Cross-project readers and the bead store assume `ddx-\\u003c8hex\\u003e` prefix matching for tooling (filtering, GraphQL adapters, MCP responses) — these long IDs may sort or filter unexpectedly.\\n- The IDs encode ephemeral attempt timestamps, which leaks worker-internal state into a permanent identifier.\\n- 12 such beads exist in the tracker as of this filing (grep `.execute-bead-wt-` in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`).\\n\\n### Layer 2 (design question — separate decision)\\n\\nEven if the IDs were correctly formed `ddx-\\u003c8hex\\u003e`, it is not obvious that `ddx bead create` from inside an execute-bead worker is the right pattern at all. Two alternatives the design has not articulated a position on:\\n\\n**(a) Append to the parent bead.** When the worker discovers sub-tasks, it appends them as structured items in the parent's description, notes, or a new `kind:discovered-subtask` event. The parent bead becomes the durable record of \\\"what this run found\\\"; future runs (or the operator) can decompose if/when the work warrants its own tracker entries.\\n\\n**(b) Surface via the execute-bead result.** The agent emits a structured `discovered_subtasks: [...]` field on result.json; the loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.\\n\\n**(c) Create new beads in-band (current behavior).** The worker calls `ddx bead create` directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue if the agent over-decomposes; complicates \\\"what was THIS run's contribution\\\" auditing.\\n\\nToday's behavior is (c) but undocumented. The 11 read-coverage children landing as a single agent's decomposition is exactly the failure mode (c) makes easy: one analysis bead spawned 11 implementation beads in a single run, all P0, all without operator review. The work may be valid — but the pattern needs a position.\\n\\n## In-scope outcomes\\n\\nThis bead's job is to surface both layers; the implementation work decomposes into follow-ups.\\n\\n1. **Concrete fix for layer 1**: bead-id resolver should normalize worktree paths to the real project root before allocating an ID. Test: `ddx bead create` invoked with cwd=`\\u003crepo\\u003e/.execute-bead-wt-...` produces `ddx-\\u003c8hex\\u003e`, not `.execute-bead-wt-...-\\u003crandom\\u003e`.\\n2. **Mass repair for the 12 existing malformed beads**: either rewrite their IDs to fresh `ddx-\\u003c8hex\\u003e` (with a redirect or alias map for any external references), or close them as deferred and re-file with proper IDs. New unit test in `cli/internal/bead/` (or `cli/cmd/`) that simulates a bead-create call from `cwd=\\u003cproject\\u003e/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-XXXXX-\\u003ctimestamp\\u003e-\\u003crandom\\u003e` and asserts the new bead's `id` field matches `^ddx-[0-9a-f]{8}$`. Tool calls stream as they happen, costs accrue visibly, and the conversation persists so I can come back to it.\\n\\n**Acceptance criteria (written for Option A; rewrite if design note chooses B/C/D):**\\n\\n1. **Design note landed.** One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the chosen option, the answers to the six subsidiary questions, and the message/thread schema. Implementation PR links to it.\\n\\n2. **Thread model.** Chat threads persist under `.ddx/chat/\\u003cthread-id\\u003e/{manifest.json,messages.jsonl}`. One thread per project per operator by default; operators can create named threads. Messages.jsonl is append-only, one JSON object per message (user or assistant), with `sessionId` linking each assistant turn back to an entry in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`).\\n\\n3. **GraphQL surface.** New resolvers:\\n   - Query: `chatThreads(projectId)`, `chatThread(id)`, `chatMessages(threadId, since?)`.\\n   - Mutation: `chatThreadCreate(projectId, title?)`, `chatSend(threadId, message, harness?, profile?)` → kicks off a session via `service.Execute`.\\n   - Subscription: `chatStream(threadId)` → emits assistant message chunks + tool call events (same event vocabulary as `workers.recentEvents`).\\n\\n4. **Home page UI.** Project home page gains a chat pane below the actions section. Pane shows thread list, active thread messages, input box, harness/profile picker. Tool calls render inline within assistant messages. Streaming updates as chunks arrive.\\n\\n5. **Session linkage.** Each assistant turn creates one session in the sessions index. Clicking an assistant message shows the session id and a link to the session detail (`/sessions/\\u003cid\\u003e`). Chat thread header shows cumulative session count + total cost for the thread.\\n\\n6. **Concurrency.** Two browsers open on the same thread see each other's messages within 2s (subscription-driven). Two operators on different threads in the same project do not interfere.\\n\\n7. **Interrupt.** The in-flight assistant turn can be cancelled via a Stop button; server sends the harness the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop \\u003cid\\u003e` for the in-flight session. The thread records a cancelled turn, does not try to auto-resume.\\n\\n8. **Cost guardrail (soft).** If the cumulative thread cost passes a configurable ceiling (`.ddx/config.yaml: chat.cost_ceiling_usd`, default unset), the UI warns but does not block. Hard block is a follow-up.\\n\\n9. **Auth posture.**\\n   - Per design note. If the chosen posture is \\\"single-operator localhost / tsnet only for v1,\\\" the chat routes are served unauthenticated behind tsnet and the design note records the gap to close before broader deployment.\\n   - A small middleware gate is in place even for v1 so adding auth later is schema-local, not route-local.\\n\\n10. **Playwright e2e.**\\n    - Seeds a project with zero chat threads.\\n    - Navigates to project home, asserts the chat pane renders with \\\"Start a new thread\\\" affordance.\\n    - Starts a thread, sends a short prompt, simulates a streamed session response with one tool call, asserts chunks appear in order and the session id link is present.\\n    - Opens a second browser context on the same thread, asserts both see new messages within 2s.\\n    - Cancels an in-flight turn, asserts the cancelled state is persisted.\\n\\n11. **Depends on.**\\n    - `ddx-2513f08c` (home page reachable from sidebar) — hard dependency.\\n    - `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) — hard dependency for the session linkage + cost rollup.\\n\\n12. **Explicitly deferred.** PTY integration (Option B / pop-out terminal from Option C) is its own bead, filed only if the Option A rollout surfaces real interactive-steering needs.\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"9c29b5fe9cb5e06c3a0f1ca3d8133f8766b59259\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-22T20:51:38.374211227Z\",\"description\":\"## Observed / Motivation\\n\\nUser wants to \\\"lift work out of the TUI entirely and allow non-tmux users to work remotely.\\\" Today the only way to prompt the DDx agent stack interactively is a terminal session. The web UI is read-mostly: dispatch three actions (drain/align/checks) and review results. There is no surface that lets an operator say \\\"go do X\\\" against the project in natural language from a browser.\\n\\nRelated UX: the project home page already exists (`/nodes/.../projects/\\u003cid\\u003e/`), renders queue summary + actions, and is the natural host for a chat affordance. First the home page needs to be reachable from the sidebar (`ddx-2513f08c`) — this bead depends on that work.\\n\\n## The tension\\n\\nThe DDx harness abstraction (`cli/internal/agent/service_run.go`, `service.Execute`) is designed for **non-interactive** use: prompt in, structured result out, optional streaming. This is the right shape for queue-draining, quorum, evaluation, and automation. It is **not** the shape of a TUI conversation with tab completion, mid-run steering, and back-channel signals.\\n\\nAn operator-facing chat can land on either side of that line. The bead records all four options so the design phase can pick one deliberately.\\n\\n## Options (design phase decides, recommended path noted)\\n\\n### Option A — Chat over the harness abstraction (RECOMMENDED starting point)\\n\\n- Chat UI posts a prompt → server dispatches a session via `service.Execute` (or a wrapper) → streams tool calls + response chunks back over a GraphQL subscription.\\n- Message history is persisted as a thread; each user turn = one new session. Responses reuse the existing `recentEvents` stream shape already used on the workers detail page.\\n- Works with every harness today because the harness abstraction is what it is. claude, codex, agent (embedded) all participate.\\n- **Trade-off:** no mid-run steering, no tab completion, no live back-and-forth inside one agent invocation.\\n- Matches the user's stated target audience (non-TUI users who want remote browser access). Those users aren't trying to steer a TUI — they're trying to leave it behind.\\n\\n### Option B — PTY gateway per harness\\n\\n- Server owns a PTY pool. Each chat thread attaches to one harness process (claude/codex/etc.) over a WebSocket; browser renders xterm.js.\\n- Full interactivity, including TUI steering.\\n- **Trade-off:** PTY lifecycle, auth, resource limits, tab-close-loses-session semantics, multi-user access control — all real. The harness abstraction is bypassed for this mode, which creates a second execution path to maintain.\\n\\n### Option C — Hybrid (chat-first + pop-out terminal)\\n\\n- Option A is the primary surface. When a chat reaches a point that needs interactive steering, a \\\"Open terminal\\\" escape hatch attaches a PTY to the current harness session.\\n- **Trade-off:** more surface area, but honest about both use cases. Most sessions stay on the cheap path.\\n\\n### Option D — Web-tmux / ttyd wrapper\\n\\n- Embed the existing terminal tooling directly: ttyd or xterm.js over a tmux socket owned by the server.\\n- **Trade-off:** fastest to ship, zero harness work, but the UX is a terminal in a browser — not \\\"chat.\\\" Requires tmux. Doesn't serve the stated \\\"non-tmux users\\\" audience.\\n\\n### Recommendation\\n\\nStart with **A**. If the design note documents real interactive-steering needs we can't paper over, the right follow-up is **C** (A + PTY escape hatch), not B (PTY-only).\\n\\n## Scope of this bead\\n\\n- Design note in `docs/helix/02-design/` locking the option, the message schema, persistence model, and auth posture. Implementation PR links to it.\\n- First implementation pass: Option A.\\n- Home page gains a chat pane as its primary interaction surface, alongside the existing queue summary + actions (which stay — they're quick-dispatch affordances, not replaced by chat).\\n\\n## Subsidiary questions the design note must answer\\n\\n1. **Message persistence.** Are chat threads per-project or per-user? Stored where (`.ddx/chat/\\u003cthread-id\\u003e/` or elsewhere)? Is history shared across operators on the same project, or isolated per operator identity?\\n2. **Harness selection.** Does the chat pick a harness per-thread (picker in the UI) or per-message (re-route each turn)? Default to profile-based selection?\\n3. **Cost accountability.** Every chat message spawns a session. Sessions are already tracked in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). The chat thread should surface cumulative cost to the operator so they don't blow budget unknowingly.\\n4. **Tool execution scope.** Chat prompts will trigger agent tool calls that read/write the project tree. Is the chat session bounded to the same permissions as `ddx agent run`? Does it run in the project worktree, a worktree per thread, a scratch dir? Default safe answer: same worktree as `ddx agent run` (user trusts ddx running anyway), but design note records it.\\n5. **Concurrency.** What happens if two operators have the chat open on the same project and both send messages? Default: independent threads, each with its own session — no shared state.\\n6. **Auth.** Today the server assumes single-operator localhost. Remote use implies some identity concept. Design note picks a posture: delegate to reverse proxy, ship a simple token, or punt and require tsnet + single-operator for v1.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Full PTY implementation (Option B). Follow-up bead if A+hybrid doesn't suffice.\\n- Cross-project chat.\\n- Voice input.\",\"events_attachment\":\"ddx-88e91fdb/events.jsonl\",\"execute-loop-no-changes-count\":1,\"feature\":\"FEAT-008\",\"id\":\"ddx-88e91fdb\",\"issue_type\":\"epic\",\"labels\":[\"feat-008\",\"feat-006\",\"chat\",\"remote-ops\",\"design\"],\"priority\":2,\"session_id\":\"eb-45976f78\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"Add chat interface to project home page: prompt ddx remotely without the TUI\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-28T22:40:26.03095778Z\"}\n.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:454:{\"acceptance\":\"The design stack defines a single-project loop that watches execution-ready beads, consumes the generic DDx execution validator, executes beads in isolated worktrees, runs required post-run checks, preserves failed iterations with explicit bead/result state, lands successful runs by rebase+fast-forward, and is explicitly scoped for first release without depending on multi-project or multi-host server behavior\",\"archived_at\":\"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-10T05:45:53Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"eitri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"1032782\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"20909fc7e6454ccddf3214da04d411c43cb9f94f\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-10T02:23:04.43034964Z\",\"dependencies\":[{\"issue_id\":\"ddx-8bb9af7c\",\"depends_on_id\":\"ddx-5448b39c\",\"type\":\"blocks\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-10T02:23:17Z\"}],\"description\":\"\\u003ccontext-digest\\u003e\\nReview area: execute-bead automation and single-project worker supervision. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, SD-012, the execute-bead contract bead ddx-5448b39c, and the requirement to ship a tested single-project loop before broader multi-project or multi-host server work.\\n\\u003c/context-digest\\u003e\\nDesign the long-running single-project loop that watches for execution-ready beads and drives `ddx agent execute-bead` safely.\\n\\n## Goals\\n1. Define a single-project loop that monitors one project queue for execution-ready beads using the generic DDx execution validator rather than HELIX-specific hidden policy\\n2. Define the exact state machine: claim/select bead, validate structural executability, execute in an isolated worktree, run post-run validity checks / required executions, classify outcome, and continue scanning\\n3. Make success and failure outcomes explicit: on failed execution or failed post-run validity checks, mark the bead/result accordingly and preserve the iteration/evidence exactly as the execute-bead contract specifies; on successful validation, land by rebase + fast-forward, reset the worker worktree to the updated branch tip, and continue\\n4. Keep the first shipped loop single-project scoped; do not require multi-project discovery, cross-project scheduling, or multi-host coordination in this bead\\n5.
